papers.title: Hypervelocity stars in the Gaia era: Runaway B stars beyond the velocity limit of classical ejection mechanisms
papers.author_names: Andreas Irrgang Simon Kreuzer Ulrich Heber

abstract.text: Young massive stars in the halo are assumed to be runaway stars from the Galactic disk. Possible ejection scenarios are binary supernova ejections (BSE) or dynamical ejections from star clusters (DE). Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are extreme runaway stars that are potentially unbound from the Galaxy. Powerful acceleration mechanisms such as the tidal disruption of a binary system by a supermassive black hole (SMBH) are required to produce them. Therefore, HVSs are believed to originate in the Galactic center (GC), the only place known to host an SMBH. The second Gaia data release (DR2) offers the opportunity of studying HVSs in an unprecedented manner. We revisit some of the most interesting high-velocity stars, that is, 15 stars for which proper motions with the Hubble Space Telescope were obtained in the pre-Gaia era, to unravel their origin. By carrying out kinematic analyses based on revised spectrophotometric distances and proper motions from Gaia DR2, kinematic properties were obtained that help constrain the spatial origins of these stars. Stars that were previously considered (un)bound remain (un)bound in Galactic potentials favored by Gaia DR2 astrometry. For nine stars (five candidate HVSs plus all four radial velocity outliers), the GC can be ruled out as spatial origin at least at $2\\\\sigma$ confidence level, suggesting that a large portion of the known HVSs are disk runaway stars launched close to or beyond Galactic escape velocities. The fastest star in the sample, HVS3, is confirmed to originate in the Large Magellanic Cloud. Because the ejection velocities of five of our non-GC stars are close to or above the upper limits predicted for BSE and DE, another powerful dynamical ejection mechanism (e.g., involving massive perturbers such as intermediate-mass black holes) is likely to operate in addition to the three classical scenarios mentioned above.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Gaia DR2 proper motions combined with revised spectrophotometric distances reveal that nine of 15 high-velocity B-type stars—five candidate hypervelocity stars and four radial velocity outliers—cannot originate from the Galactic center at 2σ confidence, ruling out tidal disruption by a supermassive black hole. The fastest star, HVS 3, is confirmed to originate in the Large Magellanic Cloud, and five non-GC stars exceed the predicted ejection velocities of classical binary supernova or dynamical ejection mechanisms, suggesting the need for additional dynamical ejection processes such as those involving intermediate-mass black holes or massive perturbers.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity stars in the Gaia era: Runaway B stars beyond the velocity limit of classical ejection mechanisms

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: A large fraction of high-velocity stars (HVSs) originate from the Galactic disk rather than the Galactic Center, with kinematic modeling showing that five unbound and four bound HVSs have ejection velocities inconsistent with GC origins, suggesting a previously unrecognized disk-ejection mechanism. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel astrometric and kinematic analysis using Gaia data to resolve the origin of hypervelocity stars, particularly challenging the Hills mechanism by demonstrating that stars like HE 0437-5439 likely originated in the Large Magellanic Cloud rather than the Galactic center, thereby solving the long-standing problem of unbound stars with implausible Galactic trajectories. Stellar properties: This section confirms the main-sequence nature of the 14 program stars through consistent atmospheric parameters and rotational velocities, revises their distances based on spectrophotometric analysis, and finds generally good agreement between HST and Gaia proper motions—except for the outlier B711, which shows discrepancies requiring further kinematic investigation. LMC origin of HVS 3: Revised photometric and kinematic analyses support an origin of HVS 3 in the LMC, with a spectrophotometric distance of 62.3 ± 7.7 kpc and consistent trajectory modeling that strengthens the earlier hypothesis. Galactic mass models and stellar trajectories: The paper proposes a Monte Carlo-based method that uses three Milky Way mass models to numerically integrate stellar trajectories backward in time, assessing stellar binding to the Galaxy and constraining their origins and ejection velocities by comparing Galactic rest-frame velocities to local escape velocities.

abstract: Young massive stars in the halo are assumed to be runaway stars from the Galactic disk.
Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) are extreme runaway stars that are potentially unbound from the Galaxy.
We revisit some of the most interesting high-velocity stars, that is, 15 stars for which proper motions with the Hubble Space Telescope were obtained in the pre-Gaia era, to unravel their origin.
Stars that were previously considered (un)bound remain (un)bound in Galactic potentials favored by Gaia DR2 astrometry.
known HVSs are disk runaway stars launched close to or beyond Galactic escape velocities.
title: Hypervelocity stars in the Gaia era: Runaway B stars beyond the velocity limit of classical ejection

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper analyzes 15 specific high-velocity stars with revised spectrophotometric distances and Gaia DR2 proper motions. Provides kinematic analysis of individual objects.

papers.title: On measuring the Galactic dark matter halo with hypervelocity stars
papers.author_names: O. Contigiani E. M. Rossi T. Marchetti

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) travel from the Galactic Centre across the dark matter halo of the Milky Way, where they are observed with velocities in excess of the Galactic escape speed. Because of their quasi-radial trajectories, they represent a unique probe of the still poorly constrained dark matter component of the Galactic potential. In this paper, we present a new method to produce such constraints. Our likelihood is based on the local HVS density obtained by back-propagating the observed phase space position and quantifies the ejection probability along the orbit. To showcase our method, we apply it to simulated Gaia samples of $\\\\sim200$ stars in three realistic Galactic potentials with dark matter components parametrized by spheroidal NFW profiles. We find that individual HVSs exhibit a degeneracy in the scale mass-scale radius plane ($M_s-r_s$) and are able to measure only the combination $\\\\alpha = M_s/r_s^2$. Likewise, a degeneracy is also present between $\\\\alpha$ and the spheroidal axis-ratio $q$. In the absence of observational errors, we show the whole sample can nail down both parameters with {\\\\it sub-per cent} precision (about $1\\\\%$ and $0.1\\\\%$ for $\\\\alpha$ and $q$ respectively) with no systematic bias. This remarkable power to constrain deviations from a symmetric halo is a consequence of the Galactocentric origin of HVSs. To compare our results with other probes, we break the degeneracy in the scale parameters and impose a mass-concentration relation. The result is a competitive precision on the virial mass $M_{200}$ of about $10\\\\%$.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper introduces a novel statistical method to constrain the dark matter halo of the Milky Way using hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by back-propagating their observed phase space positions to infer ejection probabilities along orbits. It demonstrates that individual HVSs exhibit degeneracies between scale mass ($M_s$), scale radius ($r_s$), and axis ratio ($q$), allowing only the combination $\alpha = M_s / r_s^2$ to be uniquely determined, while the full halo shape (via $q$) can be resolved at sub-percent precision when the full sample is analyzed—due to the Galactocentric origin of HVSs. The method achieves ~1% precision on $\alpha$ and ~0.1% on $q$ in ideal conditions, and when a mass-concentration relation is imposed, yields ~10% precision on the virial mass $M_{200}$, outperforming other dynamical probes in constraining halo shape and mass.

source_fields.title: On measuring the Galactic dark matter halo with hypervelocity stars

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method using the kinematics of the Sagittarius stellar stream to constrain the mass and shape of the Milky Way's dark matter halo without relying on assumptions about initial conditions or steady-state configurations, thereby addressing the long-standing problem of significant discrepancies in halo mass and shape measurements across the literature. Likelihood function: This section presents a likelihood analysis of hypervelocity stars to constrain the mass and scale radius of the Galactic dark matter halo in the $M_s$-$r_s$ plane, classifying stars based on their ability to constrain halo parameters and identifying those most effective for measuring the halo's properties. Distribution function: The distribution function of hypervelocity stars is derived as a time integral of the ejection rate and survival function, weighted by the backward trajectory in phase space, under the assumption of a stationary ejection process and volume-conserving dynamics. Appendix B Triaxial halo: This appendix demonstrates that in a triaxial dark matter halo, nearly all hypervelocity stars provide measurable constraints on the halo shape, leading to a precision of 0.49% in shape parameter inference, comparable to spheroidal halos when accounting for covariance. Simulated HVS catalogues: This section presents a parametrization of the ejection rate distribution for hypervelocity stars (HVSs) produced via the Hills mechanism, incorporating velocity, mass, and position dependencies with a velocity cutoff $v_0(m)$ derived from Monte Carlo simulations and fitted analytic forms. Discussion and conclusions: This study demonstrates that approximately 200 hypervelocity stars can provide unbiased, sub-percent uncertainty measurements of the NFW dark matter halo parameters—specifically the axis ratio $q$ and scale parameter $\\alpha = M_s/r_s^2$—through a likelihood pipeline that constrains ejection dynamics and orbital propagation, yielding tight, robust constraints even under idealized assumptions. Appendix A Two-body encounters: This appendix shows that two-body encounters with the Galactic stellar distribution induce negligible velocity deflections on hypervelocity star orbits, with perturbations small enough that they do not significantly affect the ejection conditions inferred in the main analysis.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) travel from the Galactic Centre across the dark matter halo of the Milky Way, where they are observed with velocities in excess of the Galactic escape speed.
To showcase our method, we apply it to simulated Gaia samples of $\\\\sim200$ stars in three realistic Galactic potentials with dark matter components parametrized by spheroidal NFW profiles.
title: On measuring the Galactic dark matter halo with hypervelocity stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Methodology paper presenting statistical method to constrain dark matter halo using simulated Gaia samples. No actual observational stellar data presented.

papers.title: The high velocity stars in the Local Stellar Halo from Gaia and LAMOST
papers.author_names: Cuihua Du Hefan Li Shuai Liu Thomas Donlon Heidi Jo Newberg

abstract.text: Based on the first Gaia data release and spectroscopy from the LAMOST Data Release 4, we study the kinematics and chemistry of the local halo stars. The halo stars are identified kinematically with a relative speed of at least 220 km s$^{-1}$ with respect to the local standard of rest. In total, 436 halo stars are identified. From this halo sample, 16 high velocity (HiVel) stars are identified. We studied the metallicity and [$\\\\alpha$/Fe] distribution of these HiVel stars. Though most of HiVel stars are metal-poor, there are several stars that have metallicity above $-0.5$ dex. To understand the origin of high velocity stars, we evolve the trajectory of the star backwards along the orbit in our adopted Galaxy potential model to determine the orbital parameters and assess whether the star could have originated in the Galactic center. We found that some high velocity stars could have originated from the Galactic center, but other stars were probably kicked up from the Galactic disk.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] The study identifies 16 high-velocity (HiVel) stars in the Local Stellar Halo using Gaia proper motions and LAMOST spectroscopy, finding that most are metal-poor but several have metallicities above [-0.5] dex, suggesting possible disk origins. By evolving their orbits backward in a Galactic potential model, the authors find that some HiVel stars likely originated in the Galactic center via dynamical ejections, while others were likely kicked from the Galactic disk, supporting models of in situ runaway star formation and challenging the assumption that all HiVel stars are accreted from dwarf galaxies.

source_fields.title: The high velocity stars in the Local Stellar Halo from Gaia and LAMOST

source_fields.sections_text: 2. Data: This section describes how the Gaia DR1 and LAMOST surveys provide complementary 5D astrometric and 6D kinematic (including radial velocity and metallicity) data for studying stellar kinematics and chemistry in the solar neighborhood. 1. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel approach by combining kinematic and chemical abundance analyses of late-type high-velocity stars to constrain their origins, specifically testing whether metal-rich HiVel stars formed in the disk and were dynamically ejected, thereby addressing the problem of distinguishing between disk-origin and accretion-origin models for Galactic halo formation. 5. Conclusions and summary: Analysis of local halo and high-velocity stars from Gaia and LAMOST data reveals a significant fraction of metal-rich halo stars with diverse α-element abundances and orbital properties, with some high-velocity stars originating from the Galactic disk rather than the Galactic center. 3. The local halo sample stars: This section demonstrates that kinematically defined halo stars include metal-rich stars with disk-like metallicities, indicating that metallicity alone cannot distinguish halo from disk stars and that thick disk contamination must be quantified using velocity distributions. 4. High Velocity Stars in the Local Stellar Halo: This section describes the selection and characterization of 16 high-velocity stars in the local stellar halo with space velocities exceeding 300 km s⁻¹, showing diverse spatial distributions and velocities indicative of multiple origins.

abstract: Based on the first Gaia data release and spectroscopy from the LAMOST Data Release 4, we study the kinematics and chemistry of the local halo stars.
The halo stars are identified kinematically with a relative speed of at least 220 km s$^{-1}$ with respect to the local standard of rest. In total, 436 halo stars are identified.
From this halo sample, 16 high velocity (HiVel) stars are identified. We studied the metallicity and [$\\\\alpha$/Fe] distribution of these HiVel stars.
Though most of HiVel stars are metal-poor, there are several stars that have metallicity above $-0.5$ dex.
We found that some high velocity stars could have originated from the Galactic center, but other stars were probably kicked up from the Galactic disk.
title: The high velocity stars in the Local Stellar Halo from Gaia and LAMOST

catalog_assessment.evidence: Identifies 436 halo stars and 16 high-velocity stars from Gaia DR1 and LAMOST DR4. Provides kinematic and chemical analysis of specific objects with orbital calculations.
